summary:
The Qin Construction of the Great Wall (221–206 BC) was [[event:3141|Qin Shi Huang]]'s massive engineering project connecting and extending earlier border walls to create a continuous defensive barrier against nomadic incursions from the north — a project that consumed enormous resources and became a symbol of both Chinese civilization's ambitions and its capacity for state-organized labor.

Earlier Zhou-era states had built independent walls along their northern frontiers. After unifying China, Qin Shi Huang ordered the walls connected and extended under the supervision of General Meng Tian, who commanded an army of 300,000 soldiers and over a million conscript laborers. The Qin wall ran roughly along China's northern steppe frontier, intended to prevent raids by the [[event:1133|Xiongnu Empire]]. The project was notorious for its brutality: tens of thousands of workers died and were reportedly buried within the walls themselves. The Qin wall was largely earthen and has not survived in the form visible today — the iconic stone sections of the Great Wall date mainly from the Ming dynasty (1368–1644). Nevertheless, the Qin construction established the strategic concept and administrative precedent for all subsequent wall-building.
